title: Anger at the Erasure of Indigenous Land
dek: When we witness or learn about the systematic erasure of indigenous peoples from their ancestral lands, the anger that rises isn't just political—it's cellular, a grief that lives in the body and asks us to remember what was taken, who was harmed, and what we carry forward in silence or in voice.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at indigenous land erasure is a legitimate response to ongoing historical trauma and present-day injustice. This rage carries somatic markers in the body that mirror collective grief and disconnection from place. Learning to metabolize this anger without bypassing its message creates pathways toward repair and embodied solidarity.

Have you ever felt a flash of rage reading a land acknowledgment that feels hollow, a museum placard that sanitizes genocide, or a nature trail named after a colonizer who displaced thousands? That heat in your chest, the tightness in your jaw - your body is responding to something your mind might try to rationalize away. This anger at the erasure of indigenous land is not irrational. It is not too much. It is, in fact, one of the most appropriate responses available to us when confronting centuries of dispossession, violence, and the ongoing theft of place, language, and belonging. In our experience working with people who carry anger about historical and present injustices, the body often knows before the mind catches up. The rage surfaces as a visceral protest, a refusal to let the narrative remain sanitized. And yet we are taught to soften it, to frame it more palatably, to transform justified fury into something more digestible for polite company. This is where the real harm compounds - not in the anger itself, but in the demand that we suppress it. ## The Weight of Disappeared Geographies Walk through most American cities and you will encounter streets named after generals who orchestrated massacres, parks built on burial grounds, monuments celebrating "pioneers" who were, in fact, armed settlers. The indigenous names for these places - names that carried ecological knowledge, seasonal cycles, kinship relations - have been scraped off maps and replaced with the surnames of those who profited from removal. This is not ancient history. The last federally funded Indian boarding school closed in 1978. The Navajo-Hopi land dispute continues to displace families. Sacred sites are still being bulldozed for pipelines and mining operations. When we talk about erasure, we are talking about something that is happening right now, in real time, with legal cover and public indifference. Your anger at this is your body's alarm system working correctly. Karla McLaren, who has written extensively about the intelligence of emotions, describes anger as the emotion that restores boundaries and protects what is sacred. When we feel anger at indigenous land erasure, we are responding to a profound boundary violation - the theft of home, the severing of people from the land that holds their stories, the deliberate destruction of relationships that sustained life for millennia. The body registers this as a kind of amputation, even if it is not our own land, our own people. We carry the knowledge of what it means to belong somewhere, and when we witness that belonging being systematically destroyed, the nervous system reacts. You might notice your breath becoming shallow when you read about forced relocations, your fists clenching when you see another pipeline proposal cutting through treaty land. These are not overreactions. These are embodied recognitions of harm. ## Somatic Signatures of Collective Grief Pat Ogden's work in somatic psychology offers us language for understanding how trauma lives in the body, not just as individual experience but as something passed down through generations and absorbed from the culture around us. When we feel anger about indigenous land erasure, we are often feeling something that includes but exceeds our personal experience. We are touching collective grief, historical trauma, and the unmetabolized pain of those who were silenced. In my own practice, I have noticed that this anger often carries a particular texture - it is hot, yes, but beneath the heat there is a profound sadness, a mourning for what was lost and what continues to be taken. The anger is the part that still believes repair is possible, that something can be done. The grief underneath is the part that knows how much has already been destroyed beyond recovery. Your body might hold this as tension in the shoulders, a heaviness in the chest, a restless energy in the legs that wants to move, to act, to do something. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby - it needs to be held with tenderness, not shamed or pushed away. When we feel anger at erasure, we are holding a truth that the dominant culture would prefer to forget. The anger is the part of us that refuses to forget. Consider for a moment where you feel this anger in your body right now, as you read these words. Is there a tightness in your throat, a heat in your belly, a pressure behind your eyes? These sensations are information. They are your body's way of saying: this matters. Pay attention. Do not look away. ## The Seduction of Spiritual Bypass There is a particular kind of violence that happens when we are told to transcend our anger, to focus on gratitude, to send love and light to those who are suffering while doing nothing to address the systems that cause the suffering. This is spiritual bypassing, and it is especially insidious with anger about injustice. You will encounter this in wellness spaces, in yoga studios that sit on stolen land without acknowledgment, in meditation centers that preach non-attachment while indigenous water protectors are being arrested for defending their rivers. The message is always some variation of: your anger is lowering your vibration, blocking your manifestation, keeping you from enlightenment. The message is always: be quiet. But anger, when it arises in response to genuine harm, is not something to transcend. It is something to work with, to listen to, to allow to inform action. The goal is not to eliminate the anger but to develop the capacity to be with it without either suppressing it or being consumed by it. This is where practices from [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) can support the nervous system's ability to stay present with difficult emotions - not to numb them, but to create enough internal spaciousness that we can feel them fully and respond wisely. I have seen people tie themselves in knots trying to be "spiritual" about their rage at genocide, as if the appropriate response to mass violence is to smile and affirm that everything happens for a reason. This is not spirituality. This is dissociation dressed up in new age language. Real spiritual practice includes the capacity to feel appropriate anger, to let it move through the body, and to channel it toward meaningful action rather than letting it calcify into bitterness or explode into harm. ## What the Land Remembers Stand in a place where indigenous people lived for thousands of years and were forcibly removed. Even if you do not know the specific history, you might feel something - a heaviness, a silence that is not peaceful, a sense of interruption. The land remembers what happened there. The land holds the memory of who walked on it, who prayed there, who was born and died there, who was taken away in chains or at gunpoint. This is not metaphor. Ecologists are beginning to understand that landscapes shaped by indigenous land management practices - controlled burns, selective harvesting, polyculture planting - carry those patterns for centuries after the people who created them are gone. The land remembers how it was cared for, and it also remembers the violence of removal, the sudden absence of those who tended it. When you feel anger at erasure, you are in some sense feeling on behalf of the land itself, which cannot speak in human language but which carries the imprint of what was lost. Your anger is a form of witness, a refusal to let the story be rewritten into something more comfortable for those who benefited from theft. Walk outside right now, wherever you are. Place your bare feet on the ground if you can, or press your palms against a tree, a stone, the earth itself. Feel the solidity there. Notice the life that persists - the plants growing, the insects moving, the birds singing. The land is still here. The people who were removed are still here, still fighting for their sovereignty, still protecting what remains. Your anger can be a bridge back to that reality, a way of remembering what the dominant culture wants to forget. ## Metabolizing Rage Without Bypassing Truth So what do you do with this anger? How do you hold it without letting it consume you, without turning away from it in discomfort, without weaponizing it in ways that cause more harm? First, you stop pathologizing it. Your anger at indigenous land erasure is not a problem to be solved. It is a rational response to ongoing injustice. The problem is not the anger. The problem is the injustice. Second, you give it space in your body. This might mean movement - dancing, running, hitting a pillow, shaking. It might mean sound - yelling into a pillow, humming, toning, letting the voice express what words cannot. It might mean stillness - sitting with the sensation of anger in your body, breathing into it, noticing where it lives and how it moves. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the importance of letting emotions move through the body rather than getting stuck in the mind's endless loops of analysis and justification. Third, you investigate what the anger is protecting. Beneath anger there is almost always something vulnerable - love for what is being harmed, grief for what is being lost, fear that it will continue, a deep longing for things to be different. When you can touch that vulnerable place, the anger often softens without disappearing. It becomes less reactive and more focused, less a explosion and more a sustained flame. Here is a simple practice you can do right now: Place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly. Take three slow breaths. Ask yourself: "What is this anger protecting in me?" Do not rush to answer. Let the question sit in your body. Notice what arises - images, sensations, memories, insights. Sometimes the anger is protecting your capacity to care, your refusal to become numb, your commitment to justice. Let yourself feel that. Let yourself honor it. For some people, supporting the body's stress response with supplements like [Pure Encapsulations Potassium Magnesium Citrate (180 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0017I29HG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help maintain physiological balance while processing difficult emotions. This is not about numbing - it is about giving your nervous system the resources it needs to stay present without going into fight-flight-freeze. ## From Rage to Relationship The ultimate question is not whether you should feel anger at indigenous land erasure - of course you should - but rather how that anger can become a doorway into deeper relationship with the land you live on and the people whose ancestral territory it is. This means learning whose land you are on. Not just reciting a land acknowledgment at the start of a meeting, but actually learning the history, the languages, the current struggles of the indigenous nations whose territory you occupy. It means finding out what they need and how you can support their sovereignty - not in abstract, feel-good ways, but in concrete, material ways. Money. Political action. Showing up. It means examining your own relationship to land, to place, to belonging. How do you inhabit the ground beneath your feet? Do you know the watershed you live in, the plants that are native here, the animals whose habitat you share? Have you built relationships with the place itself, or do you move through it as if it were backdrop, scenery, real estate? The anger at erasure can crack you open to a different way of being in the world - one that recognizes land not as property but as kin, not as resource but as relationship. This is not about appropriating indigenous worldviews. It is about letting your own capacity for connection and reciprocity come back online after generations of cultural conditioning that taught you to see the world as dead matter to be exploited. There are indigenous-led organizations doing land back work, fighting pipeline construction, protecting water, revitalizing languages, teaching traditional ecological knowledge. Your anger becomes meaningful when it moves you to support that work, to use whatever resources and privileges you have in service of repair and justice. ## Anger as Information, Not Identity One of the traps people fall into with righteous anger is making it central to their identity. The anger becomes who they are rather than information they are working with. This is when you see people who are perpetually outraged, who cannot experience joy or beauty or rest because they are so consumed by the injustices they are tracking. This is not sustainable, and ultimately it does not serve the cause. The Buddhist teacher Thich Nhat Hanh offered a practice he called "taking care of the angry person inside." He suggested that when anger arises, we recognize it, greet it, and care for it the way we would care for a suffering child. We do not indulge it or let it run wild, but we also do not shame it or push it away. We acknowledge its presence and its reasons, and we create space for it to transform. This transformation does not mean the anger disappears. It means the anger becomes workable, becomes something that can inform action without distorting perception. You can feel angry about indigenous land erasure and also feel joy when you see indigenous youth reclaiming their languages. You can feel angry about ongoing injustice and also feel gratitude for the land that sustains you. These are not contradictions. They are the full range of human response to a complex reality. The practice is to notice when anger tips over into something that is harming you - when it becomes chronic tension that never releases, when it isolates you from connection, when it makes you cruel or rigid or unable to see nuance. These are signs that the anger needs attention, needs to be metabolized more fully. Resources like [Bach Vine Non-Alcohol (10 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BM4XBYKL?tag=spankyspinola-20) are traditionally used to support emotional flexibility when we find ourselves stuck in a single emotional register, unable to access the full spectrum of response. According to the CDC's guidance on stress and the body, chronic activation of the stress response without adequate recovery time can lead to serious health consequences. This is true for individual trauma and for collective trauma. We need practices that allow the nervous system to discharge, to rest, to come back to baseline before the next activation. ## The Practice of Remembering What does it mean to remember in a culture built on forgetting? To remember not as an intellectual exercise but as a bodily practice, a way of living in relationship with what came before and what continues now? It means speaking the names that were erased. Learning them, teaching them to your children, using them in daily life. It means questioning every monument, every street name, every historical marker that celebrates colonization as progress. It means reading indigenous authors, supporting indigenous artists, buying from indigenous-owned businesses, following indigenous activists and water protectors on social media so their voices are strengthened rather than drowned out. It means sitting with discomfort. If you are a non-indigenous person living on stolen land, there is no way to be comfortable with that reality. The discomfort is appropriate. The question is whether you will numb it or let it motivate you toward repair. Over the years I've seen people approach this work in two ways. Some treat it as a project - they learn the facts, they post the land acknowledgments, they check the box and move on. Others let it change them. They let the knowledge of what was taken and what continues to be taken break their hearts open, and they let that heartbreak inform how they live, how they spend their money, how they vote, how they raise their children, how they relate to the ground beneath their feet. The second way is harder. It requires ongoing discomfort, ongoing questioning, ongoing willingness to be changed by what you learn. But it is also the only way that leads to genuine solidarity rather than performative allyship. For deeper reflection on the relationship between place, soul, and belonging, [Care of the Soul - Thomas Moore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0060921269?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a contemplative approach to living with more presence and reverence, though Moore writes from a different context than indigenous land ethics. ## When Anger Meets Action There is a moment in the process of working with anger when it crystallizes into clarity. You stop spinning in rage and grief and you start moving with purpose. You know what you are angry about, you know why it matters, and you know what you are going to do about it. This is the moment when anger becomes fuel rather than fire. When it becomes the energy that gets you out of bed to go to a city council meeting about a development proposal on sacred land. When it becomes the courage to have difficult conversations with family members who do not understand why you care about "ancient history." When it becomes the commitment to learn a language, to donate monthly to a land back fund, to show up at a protest in the cold rain because your body will not let you stay home. The action does not have to be grand. It can be small, local, personal. It can be learning to identify native plants and planting them in your yard. It can be buying your coffee from an indigenous-owned roaster. It can be reading a book about the specific history of the land you live on and then telling that story to someone else. The size of the action matters less than the consistency, the commitment to let your anger inform your choices rather than consuming you from the inside. You might explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) to better understand your own patterns of emotional processing and stress response, which can inform how you channel anger into sustainable action rather than burnout. ## Three Questions Readers Ask ### How do I feel anger about indigenous land erasure without it consuming my daily life? The key is rhythm - allowing yourself to feel the anger fully when it arises, creating time and space to process it through your body, and then returning to the other aspects of your life. You are not required to be angry every moment. You are allowed to rest, to experience joy, to tend to your immediate relationships and responsibilities. The practice is developing the capacity to move between states - to feel the anger when it is present, to let it inform your actions, and then to release it so you can be present for what else needs your attention. Think of it like tending a fire: you feed it when needed, you use its heat for warmth and cooking, but you do not let it burn the house down. ### Is it appropriative for me to feel angry about indigenous land erasure if I am not indigenous? No. Anger at injustice is not appropriation - it is an appropriate human response to harm. What matters is what you do with that anger. If you use it to center yourself, to make yourself the hero of someone else's story, to speak over indigenous voices, then you have crossed into harmful territory. But if you use it to strengthen indigenous voices, to support indigenous-led movements, to examine your own complicity and make different choices, then your anger is in service of justice rather than ego. The question to keep asking yourself is: who is this anger serving? If the answer is your own sense of righteousness, recalibrate. If the answer is the people who are actually fighting for their land and sovereignty, then you are on the right path. ### What is the difference between feeling anger and taking action? Feeling anger is the first step, not the final destination. Anger without action becomes bitterness, chronic stress, and eventually burnout or numbness. Action without anger can become performative, disconnected from the genuine stakes. The sweet spot is when the two work together - when you feel the anger in your body as information about what matters, and then you let that information guide you toward concrete choices and actions. This might look like: feeling anger when you learn about a pipeline cutting through treaty land, then researching the indigenous-led organizations fighting it, then donating money or time or showing up to a protest. The anger is the spark. The action is what you build from that spark. For more on translating emotional energy into meaningful change, you might find [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore the intersection of feeling and doing. ## Opening Outward On [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), we often write about the ways that personal transformation and collective healing are not separate projects but different expressions of the same commitment to living with more awareness, more courage, more love. The anger you feel at indigenous land erasure is part of that commitment. It is your heart's refusal to accept injustice as inevitable. It is your body's insistence that what happened matters, that what is happening now matters, that the relationships between people and land and belonging are sacred and worth defending. What if your anger is not a problem but a portal? What if it is the very thing that can crack open your heart enough to feel genuine connection to the land beneath your feet, to the people whose ancestors walked here long before you, to the more-than-human world that has been harmed by the same systems that displaced indigenous peoples? What if your anger is the thread that, when you pull it, unravels the whole fabric of separation and alienation that modern life is built on, revealing the older truth underneath - that we all belong to the earth, that we are all responsible for repair, that the work of justice and the work of love are one and the same? Your anger is not the end of the story. It is the beginning of a different way of being in the world, one that refuses erasure, one that remembers, one that acts. The question is not whether you will feel it - you already do. The question is what you will build from it, how you will let it shape you, and whether you will let it move you closer to the world you long for, the one where land and people and justice are no longer severed from each other but woven back together, slowly, imperfectly, with great love and great rage and great hope. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on anger at the erasure of indigenous land, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
